content: Vaccines work by teaching your immune system how to recognize and fight harmful viruses or bacteria without making you sick. When you receive a vaccine, it introduces a safe part of the germ—often a weakened or inactive piece—into your body. This “practice run” prompts your immune system to respond as if it were facing the real threat. It creates specialized cells and antibodies designed to identify and destroy the specific germ. If you ever encounter the actual infection later, your immune system is prepared to act quickly and effectively, preventing illness or reducing its severity. In essence, vaccines train your body’s natural defenses, giving you protection without the risk of serious disease. This smart preparation helps maintain your health and supports the well-being of the community by reducing the spread of infections.
